vowels.note: 6 oral vs 6 nasal vowels; the contrast is phonemic. <y> represents the high central unrounded vowel /ɨ/.

puso.name: puso (from pu 'sound' + so 'cut')
puso.note: Initial glottal stop on vowel-initial words is not written when in isolation, but is written whenever an affix or another root is attached (e.g. ára 'time' but hi'ára 'his/her time').

tilde.represents: phonologically (inherently) nasal vowels — distinct from vowels that become nasal via nasal harmony
tilde.note: On a nasal morpheme, nasality is marked on the stressed syllable and no acute stress mark is then used.

acute_accent.function: marks an oral vowel that bears primary stress when that vowel is NOT the final vowel of the morphological word. Final-stressed oral words leave the stress unwritten.

phonology_orthography.stress_rule: Native lexical roots are stressed on their final vowel. With multiple morphemes, the last inherently stressed morpheme bears the word's primary stress. Prefixes are always unstressed; suffixes/enclitics may be stressed or unstressed.

nasal_harmony_rule.core_rule: A nasal vowel or nasal consonant in a root spreads nasalization throughout the morphological word. Spread is mainly regressive (leftward) up to about three syllables; rightward spread is limited to roughly the next syllable. Voiceless stops (p, t, k) are transparent to leftward spread but are blocked by/affected by rightward spread.

spelling_alternations_triggered.mb → m: e.g. mbo- (causative) → mo- before nasal root
spelling_alternations_triggered.j → ñ: e.g. ja- (1PL.INCL.ACT) → ña-; je- (AGD) → ñe-; jo- (RECP) → ño-

affix_allomorph_table.causative: mbo-, mby-, mbu- / mo-, my-, mu-

nasal_harmony_rule.exception: Suffixes/enclitics with nasal consonants (-mi, -nte, -ma, -na) do NOT trigger leftward nasal spread to the root. Compound-root boundaries also block regressive nasalization.

orthographic_conventions: Free personal pronouns are written as separate words; person prefixes are written attached to the predicate (in this grammar; Academy orthography writes them separately).
Demonstratives, numerals, and other stressable forms are written separately from the noun/verb.
Monosyllabic postpositions are traditionally written attached; bisyllabic postpositions traditionally written separately. The grammar attaches all postpositions with '=' to reflect enclitic status.
Compound words: no space between roots; nasal tildes preserved on each nasal morpheme even when only one bears primary stress (e.g. kuãirũ 'ring' < kuã+irũ).
Reduplications written as one word or two (depending on source), glossed with ~.
g̃ is used in nasal contexts to represent [ŋ]; often spelled simply g in many texts (haguã ~ hag̃ua ~ hanguã are all attested for 'for').
Variation: tẽra and térã both attested for 'or'; ko'ág̃a / ko'ãga 'now'.

free_personal_pronouns.subject: ñande
free_personal_pronouns.example: Ñande jaestudia porã. 'We (and you) studied well.'

free_personal_pronouns.subject: ore
free_personal_pronouns.example: Ore roestudia porã. 'We (excluding you) studied well.'

free_personal_pronouns.subject: peẽ
free_personal_pronouns.example: Aagradeceterei peẽme. 'I thank you (pl.) very much.'

free_personal_pronouns.subject: ha'e(kuéra)
free_personal_pronouns.example: Ha'ekuéra ho'u mbohapy kagua. 'They drank three glasses.'

active_prefixes_areal.marks: 3 subject (sg or pl; hikuái added postverbally to disambiguate plural)

active_prefixes_areal.example: japuka 'we (and you) laugh' (ja-puka); ñag̃uahẽ 'we (and you) arrive(d)' (ña-g̃uahẽ)

inactive_prefixes_chendal.marks: 1SG (subject of stative verb / patient with transitive / possessor)
3 (i- before consonant; ij- before unstressed vowel; hi'- before stressed vowel; iñ- with nasal bases not beginning in nasal/nasal-oral C)
inactive_prefixes_chendal.example: iporã 's/he is pretty'; ijao 'his/her clothes'; hi'áva 'his/her hair'; iñakã 'his/her head'; imbarakaja 'his/her cat'
ñandekatupyry 'we (and you) are smart'; ñanerasẽ 'we (and you) cry'
pendeyvate 'you (pl.) are tall'; penememby 'you (pl.) have children (said to women)'

imperative_prefix.example: Egueru chéve. 'Bring (to) me.' / Ehupi pe ndeao. 'Pick up your clothes.'

portmanteau_prefixes.example: Rohayhu. 'I/we love you (sg.).' (ro-h-ayhu) — homophonous with 1PL.EXCL ro-

third_person_plural_marker.function: postverbal pronoun used to disambiguate plural 3rd-person subjects (since o- and i- do not distinguish number)
third_person_plural_marker.example: oikuaa hikuái 'they know'; Ndepa nandegustái ko ta'anga? — etc.

tam_markers.function: future tense / prospective; also epistemic conjecture
tam_markers.example: Ko'ẽro amba'apóta. 'I will work tomorrow.' (a-mba'apo-ta) / Mba'e carrérata piko upéva. 'What career would that be?'

tam_markers.function: negative-future replacement of -ta (in negation); also frustrative ('was about to but didn't'); also incompletive ('almost')
tam_markers.example: namba'apomo'ãi 'I will not work' (n-a-mba'apo-mo'ã-i); ohomo'ã 's/he was about to go but didn't'; Ha'amo'ã. 'I almost fell.'
Ko'ẽro amba'apóne. 'Tomorrow I will work perhaps.'

tam_markers.function: negative dubitative future ('perhaps not'), follows negative form
tam_markers.example: Name'ẽi chéne. 'I may not give (it).'
jahapota 'we are about to go'; Cheirũ omanombota hína. 'My friend is about to die.'
Rejuva'erã cherógape. 'You must come to my house.'; ¡He'u'arã! 'You have to eat it!'

tam_markers.function: past tense (often remote past); adjectivizing past
tam_markers.example: Ahava'ekue la Chákope. 'I went to the Chaco (at some point in the past).'; Ha'eningo heñói'akue táva Caballerope. 'He was born in Caballero.'

tam_markers.function: marker of direct (witnessed) evidence; often interpreted as recent past
tam_markers.example: Upe ka'aru nomýiri, te'onguetýicha kuri. 'The afternoon was still like a cemetery.'

tam_markers.function: marker of reasoned/inferred evidence (mirative; recent realization, often counterexpectation)
tam_markers.example: Ndevaléngo ra'e. 'It turned out that you were good.'

tam_markers.function: marker of long-chain reasoned evidence; distant past
tam_markers.example: Ko ógape ndaje oiko raka'e che taitachu. 'They say my great-grandfather lived in this house.'

tam_markers.function: completive aspect ('already'); often paired with prefix ja- (borrowed from Spanish 'ya') as a circumfix
tam_markers.example: og̃uahẽma 's/he has already arrived'; jaohóma 's/he already went'

tam_markers.function: totalitive (perfective) — event applies to/exhausts the whole entity
tam_markers.example: akarupa 'I ate all / I am totally finished eating'; Tahyikuéra omanomba. 'The ants all died.'
rehai hína 'you are writing'; Cheirũ omanombota hína. 'My friend is about to die.'
omba'apo hikóni 's/he is (out and about) working (intermittently)'
ajapomi 'I used to make'; Py'ỹi ahami penderógape. 'I used to go to your house often.'
ajapóva 'I am in the habit of making'
Apu'ã jepi voiete. 'I usually get up very early.'
omoañetejey 's/he reaffirms'; Ipu jeýma. 'It (the music) sounds again already.'
romomarandujevyvy va'ekue 'I warned you several times'

tam_markers.function: simultaneity ('while'); used in serial verb constructions for aspectual nuances
tam_markers.example: ijapu oikóvo 's/he keeps on lying / s/he goes around lying'
Iporã voi. 'It is (certainly) beautiful.'

tam_markers.function: veridical emphatic (VERD); affirms truth, often contrastive
tam_markers.example: Auto tujango kóa! 'But this is an old car!'
Jaipuru jevýta ndaje umi céntimo. 'They say we will use the cents again.'
Ohójeko Perurima ohechamívo peteĩ iñamígope. 'They say Perurima used to go visit a friend.'
Oĩmbariréma oũva'erã yvy apére ñandeko oiko kóa ko mba'e. (hearsay frame)
Ohenoiukáje Perurima peteĩ pyharépe Añáme. 'One night, they say, Perurima had the devil called.'

tam_markers.function: ra'e + uncertainty mbo/nipo: reinforced surprise / counterexpectation
tam_markers.example: Hãã, ava kuimba'e mbora'e! 'Ah, a man!'; omano nipo ra'e 'It turned out s/he died (contrary to what I thought).'
Ndefelínte che aipota. 'I only want you (to be) happy.'

negation.suffix_allomorphs: -i after consonant or non-i/ĩ vowel; -ri after a base ending in i or ĩ
prefix_allomorphs: nd- before vowel-initial active prefixes a-, o-
nda- (oral) / na- (nasal) before consonant-initial inactive prefixes (che-, nde-/ne-, i-/iñ-, ñande-/ñane-, ore-, pende-/pene-) and as the default
Vowel-harmonic helping vowel before consonant-initial active prefixes: nde-re- (2SG.ACT), ndo-ro- (1PL.EXCL.ACT), n-o- (3.ACT — but no helping vowel because o- is vowel-initial), etc.
Exception: 2PL.ACT pe- takes nda-pe- (with non-harmonic a)
negation.future_negation: Replace -ta with -mo'ã inside the n(d)-…-i circumfix: namba'apomo'ãi 'I will not work' (n-a-mba'apo-mo'ã-i)

1SG.ACT: namañái 'I don't look at' (nasal root maña)
ndajopíri 'I don't sting' (oral, ends in i)
namotĩri 'I don't shame' (nasal, ends in ĩ)

examples: ndachepochýi 'I am not angry' (nominal predicate negated)
Ndaipotái mba'eve. 'I don't want anything.' (negative concord)
negation.imperative_negation: ani / anína (NEG.IMP) prefixed to the verb form; e.g. Ani repoko cherehe. 'Don't touch me.'
negation.special: ne'ĩra 'not yet' is the only negative word that co-occurs with non-negated predicates: ne'ĩra reikuaa 'you don't know yet'

plural.function: plural; mostly for animate or contrastive plurality; optional with numerals/quantifiers

multitudinal_plural.function: indicates substantial quantity; can pluralize uncountables

collective_plurals.function: place where animals/people abound (mostly fossilized in toponyms)

gender_marking.method: compound with kuña 'woman' or kuimba'e 'man' (nouns are not inherently gendered)

possessive_prefixes.example: ipo 'his/her hand(s)'; ijao 'his/her clothes'; hi'áva 'his/her hair'; iñakã 'his/her head'

possession_by_juxtaposition.rule: Full-NP possessor + possessum, in that order, no marking. If possessum is a relational root, it takes r-.

examples: Chemba'etee. 'It is my very own thing.'

relational_prefixes_triform.description: A class of nouns (and some verbs/adjectives/postpositions) whose initial consonant alternates between t-, h-, r-.
relational_prefixes_triform.r_form: possessed by full-NP possessor or by 1st/2nd-person inactive prefix (POSSM)

table.r_form: nderupa 'your bed'; chesy rupa 'my mom's bed'
ñanderesa 'our eyes'; mbo'ehára resa 'the teacher's eyes'
orerova 'our face'; hembireko rova 'his wife's face'

case_postpositions.gloss: in, on, at, to (locative); marker for human direct objects; recipient/IO marker
examples: Ame'ẽta kóva chesýpe. 'I will give this to my mother.'

case_postpositions.gloss: at, about, on (oblique; required by many postpositional-complement verbs)

examples: Eho Peru jarýindive. 'Go with Pedro's grandmother.'

case_postpositions.gloss: destinative/prospective ('for', future-oriented; also dative of purpose)

examples: Cheru rupi che aikove. 'I live because of my father.'
pohã ñana rupive 'by means of medicinal plants'

case_postpositions.gloss: in front of, in the presence of (relational)

examples: nerendápe ajujevy 'I return by your side'
ka'aruete ro'y jave 'on a cold evening'
la gérra káusa 'because of the war (blame)'

word_order_and_syntax.point: Pro-drop: subject and object NPs may be omitted when person/number is recoverable from the verbal prefix. Subject drop is more common than object drop.
Basic clause order is flexible: SV/VS for intransitives; SVO, VOS, VSO all attested for transitives. When an animate object NP is present it is most commonly post-verbal (VO).
word_order_and_syntax.example: Ahecháma ichupe. 'I already saw him/her.' / Opuka ha oñembohorýnte ha'e. 'S/he laughed and just enjoyed himself.' (VS) / ojukapáta lo mitãme la fanatismo 'fanaticism will destroy the boys' (V-O-S)

word_order_and_syntax.point: Predicative and equative clauses have no copula; the two NPs are simply juxtaposed (possessor-possessum order is the same shape, disambiguated by prosody/clitics).
word_order_and_syntax.example: Ndesy mbo'ehára. 'Your mother is a teacher.' / Ndesy orembo'ehára. 'Your mother is our teacher.'

word_order_and_syntax.point: Existential/locative clauses use -ime 'be located' or oĩ 'there is'; -iko 'live, exist, be in a state' is used for being-in-a-state and habitation.
word_order_and_syntax.example: Aime ko'ápe. 'I am here.' / Oĩpa pakova? 'Are there bananas?' / Aiko porã. 'I am well.'

word_order_and_syntax.point: Yes/no questions are marked by the 2nd-position clitic =pa or =piko (no special intonation or order); content questions add an interrogative word (mba'e, máva, moõ, araka'e, mbovy…) usually plus =pa/=piko.
word_order_and_syntax.example: Ndépa Pablo? 'Are you Pablo?' / Mba'épiko jajapóta? 'What are we going to do?' / Moõpa rehose? 'Where do you want to go?'

word_order_and_syntax.point: Direct/inverse alignment on transitives: the verbal person prefix tracks the higher participant on the hierarchy 1>2>3 — active prefix if that participant is the agent, inactive prefix if the patient. Portmanteau ro- (1>2SG) and po- (1>2PL) cover 1st-on-2nd scenarios.
word_order_and_syntax.example: ainupã ichupe 'I beat him' (1>3, active) / chenupã ha'e 's/he beat me' (3>1, inactive) / Rohayhu. 'I love you (sg.).'

word_order_and_syntax.point: Negation is a circumfix nd-…-i / n-…-i wrapping the entire predicate (or even a non-predicate phrase being negated); negative concord forces indefinites to be negative when the predicate is negated.
